When the people of Israel saw the sanctuary taking shape, something remarkable happened: they gave so abundantly that the craftsmen had to ask Moses to stop them. This is not a small moment. The very people who had wavered in faith, who had crafted a golden calf while Moses was on the mountain, now brought offerings morning after morning until their generosity outpaced the work itself. The skilled workers—Bezalel, Oholiab, and all whose hearts the LORD had stirred—left their posts to report that they had more than enough. Moses had to send a proclamation through the camp: stop giving. The abundance was complete. What does it tell us about transformed hearts that people who once demanded false gods now cannot help but give to the true God?

The remainder of Exodus 36 walks us through the meticulous construction of the tabernacle—curtains of linen and goat hair, frames of acacia wood fitted with precise geometry, silver bases and bronze clasps, gold overlay and gold rings. The details are exhaustive and might seem tedious, but they matter profoundly. Every measurement is intentional. Every material—the fine linen, the scarlet and purple, the gold and silver—carries meaning and cost. The veil that separates the holy place from the most holy place is woven with the same artistry as the entrance curtain. The structure itself testifies that God's dwelling place is not haphazard or makeshift. It is crafted with the same excellence we might expect if God Himself were directing the work—which He was, through the skill He had given His people.

What emerges from this chapter is a portrait of God's people unified in purpose and empowered by His Spirit. The craftsmen were not selected because they excelled at bureaucratic processes or survived a competitive audition. They were chosen because the LORD Himself had given them skill and stirred their hearts to use it. The people gave not because they were commanded to give in excess, but because gratitude and worship compelled them. The tabernacle that rose from their offerings and labor would be the place where God's presence dwelt among them—not in a distant heaven, but in the camp itself, accessible through prescribed means, holy and near. That same God who accepts the gifted work of human hands invites you now to offer not just material gifts, but the full allegiance of your heart and the skill of your hands, whatever those may be. He has given you what you need. The question is whether your heart, like theirs, is stirred to use it for His glory.
